title: Podman --tty breaks pipelines while docker does not
body:
### Update

This issue was initially opened with the idea I was hitting a bug in Podman, it turned out that CRLF is set by the TTY (probably from [the kernel](https://github.com/moby/moby/issues/8513#issuecomment-218044116)), and the same behavior can be experienced with Docker as well.
Moving further with some tests I found that Podman misbehaves when pipes are involved, while Docker correctly handles them.
For a quick sum up jump to https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/9718#issuecomment-799925847, avoiding all the tests which brought me there.

---

/kind bug

**Description**

Whenever I run `podman container exec/run --tty [...]` the output use CRNL (0x0d, 0x0a) instead a simple NL (0x0a) at the end of each output line on Linux.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. `podman container run -d --name test alpine:latest sleep infitely `
2. `podman container exec -t test echo a | od -c`
```
0000000   a  \r  \n
0000003
```
3. `podman container exec test echo a | od -c` (without --tty)
```
0000000   a  \n
0000002
```

same issue with `run`:

1. `podman container run --rm -t alpine:latest echo a | od -c`
```
0000000   a  \r  \n
0000003
```
#### I also test it directly via `runc`
and I doubt it's `runc` fault, as was suggested https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/3179#issuecomment-494783055:
`runc exec -t c45fa68707ef26672b545a9fc6c317f1b48b5bc5f2df2c9bec22a7eac3339604 echo a | od -c`
```
0000000   a  \n
0000002
```
`podman container exec -t c45fa68707ef26672b545a9fc6c317f1b48b5bc5f2df2c9bec22a7eac3339604 echo a | od -c`
```
0000000   a  \r  \n
0000003
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

I'm expecting to only get `\n` at the end of each output line in Linux.

**Additional information:**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.0.1
API Version:  3.0.0
Go Version:   go1.16
Git Commit:   c640670e85c4aaaff92741691d6a854a90229d8d
Built:        Sun Feb 21 16:29:46 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide?**

Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
I'm using Manjaro Linux, zsh (v. 5.8) and Tmux (v. 3.1c)

`stty -a` (zsh on my host system)

comments.body: related issues I read, but have not helped me are: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/7942, https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/8342, https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/3179.

In this related Docker issue:
https://github.com/moby/moby/issues/37366#issuecomment-401157643
it is stated that this is not a bug.
The carriage return is added by the TTY (not Docker). I guess the situation is the same for Podman.

> In this related Docker issue:
> [moby/moby#37366 (comment)](https://github.com/moby/moby/issues/37366#issuecomment-401157643)
> it is stated that this is not a bug.
> The carriage return is added by the TTY (not Docker). I guess the situation is the same for Podman.

This is very interesting, thanks. 

Can you tell me why podman behaves slightly different than docker?
```bash
$ docker run --rm --tty busybox sh -c "echo 1; echo 2" | od -c
0000000   1  \r  \n   2  \r  \n
0000006
$ docker run --rm --tty busybox sh -c "echo 1; echo 2" | cat -A
1^M$
2^M$
```

```bash
$ podman run --rm --tty busybox sh -c "echo 1; echo 2" | od -c
0000000   1  \r  \n   2  \r  \n
0000006
$ podman run --rm --tty busybox sh -c "echo 1; echo 2" | cat -A
1^M$
    2^M$
```
This messes up any output I'm trying to handle with a pipeline. Could you please tell me if you experience the same behavior??

other examples with podman:
```bash
> $0 --version | head -n 1 # bash version
GNU bash, version 5.1.0(1)-release (x86_64-pc-linux-gnu)
> echo 1
1
> podman run --rm --tty busybox sh -c "stty -onlcr; echo 1; echo 2" | cat -A
1$
  2$
    > echo 1 | cat -A
1$
> podman run --rm --tty busybox sh -c "echo 1; echo 2" | cat -A
1^M$
    2^M$
        > echo 1 | cat -A
1$
> podman run --rm --tty busybox sh -c "stty -onlcr; echo 1; echo 2" | cat -A
1$
  2$
```

with docker:
```bash
> $0 --version | head -n 1 # bash version
GNU bash, version 5.1.0(1)-release (x86_64-pc-linux-gnu)
> docker run --rm --tty busybox sh -c "stty -onlcr; echo 1; echo 2" | cat -A
1$
2$
> docker run --rm --tty busybox sh -c "echo 1; echo 2" | cat -A
1^M$
2^M$
```
same behavior with zsh:
```bash
$0 --version | head -n 1 # zsh version
zsh 5.8 (x86_64-pc-linux-gnu)
> echo 1 | cat -A
1$
> docker run --rm --tty busybox sh -c "stty -onlcr; echo 1; echo 2" | cat -A
1$
2$
> docker run --rm --tty busybox sh -c "echo 1; echo 2" | cat -A
1^M$
2^M$
> podman run --rm --tty busybox sh -c "stty -onlcr; echo 1; echo 2" | cat -A
1$
  2$
    %
> podman run --rm --tty busybox sh -c "echo 1; echo 2" | cat -A
1^M$
    2^M$
        %
```

Container engine apart, this is related to the shell. It seems once set `stty -onlcr`, from a sub-shell (or a container with access to the same TTY), that setting is then kept for subsequent commands. And TBH it sounds normal to me, since the shell and any sub-shell run within the same TTY.
```bash
> sh -c "echo 1; echo 2" | cat -A
1$
2$
> sh -c "stty -onlcr; echo 1; echo 2" | cat -A
1$
  2$
    > sh -c "echo 1; echo 2" | cat -At -A
1$
  2$
    > sh -c "stty onlcr; echo 1; echo 2" | cat -At -A
1$
2$
> sh -c "echo 1; echo 2" | cat -A
1$
2$
```
## More specific question is
Why docker behaves differently? It works ok with pipes even without touching TTY options ( `-onlcr` )
```bash
> podman run --rm --tty busybox sh -c "echo 1; echo 2" | cat -A
1^M$
    2^M$
```
```bash
> docker run --rm --tty busybox sh -c "echo 1; echo 2" | cat -A
1^M$
2^M$
```

Summarize the findings above:

```bash
> # Podman behaves weirdly with CR and pipelines
> podman run --rm --tty busybox echo | cat -A
^M$
   > # pressed enter here
> # Docker handles it correctly instead
> docker run --rm --tty busybox echo | cat -A
^M$
> # pressed enter here
> # The expected correct behavior is shown by reproducing
> # the same output with echo. (same as Docker) 
> echo -ne "\r\n" | cat -A
> ^M$
> # pressed enter here
> 
```
I think this is unrelated to all the discussions about `onlcr` (like https://github.com/moby/moby/issues/8513), on previous tests I tried to enable and disable that option and the same misbehavior occurred.

My question is simple: why Podman behaves this way? It brakes all my pipelines.
I would figure there is some bug/issue buried in podman that is causing the difference.

Why are you using -t with pipes? The intended use of -t is user facing
applications - if you’re piping output into another command you should
avoid it.

On Mon, Mar 15, 2021 at 23:56 Gianluca Gabrielli ***@***.***>
wrote:

> Summarize the findings above:
>
> > # Podman behaves weirdly with CR and pipelines> podman run --rm --tty busybox echo | cat -A
> ^M$
>    > # pressed enter here> # Docker handles it correctly instead> docker run --rm --tty busybox echo | cat -A
> ^M$> # pressed enter here> # The expected correct behavior is shown by reproducing> # the same output with echo. Same as Docker > echo -ne "\r\n" | cat -A> ^M$> # pressed enter here>
>
> Why Podman behaves this way? It brakes all my pipelines.
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you are subscribed to this thread.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/9718#issuecomment-799925847>,
> or unsubscribe
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/AB3AOCHGLGZBKM3UAEWFAR3TD3JHPANCNFSM4ZG3QIDA>
> .
>

> Why are you using -t with pipes? The intended use of -t is user facing applications - if you’re piping output into another command you should avoid it.

I'm using `podman exec` in a way similar to `toolbox`, sometimes I need to just run something not interactive like 
```
podman container exec -ti -w $(pwd) container_name echo test
```
where `-t` can be omitted, but some other times I use interactive commands like: 
```
podman container exec -ti -w $(pwd) container_name vim file
or
podman container exec -ti -w $(pwd) container_name less file
```
For the latter examples I need `--tty` option enabled. My problems arise when I do `podman container exec -ti -w $(pwd) container_name echo test | whatever`

> I would figure there is some bug/issue buried in podman that is causing the difference.

Do you know how these buried issues can be addressed?
Nope,  @baude @mheon Any ideas?

`stty -a` gives different results on Podman and Docker.

```
esjolund@laptop:~$ cat /tmp/podman
```

Can you just omit -t on output you will be piping? The flag is independent
for each exec session so there is no obligation to use it every time.

> Can you just omit -t on output you will be piping? The flag is independent for each exec session so there is no obligation to use it every time.

TBH this is not really a solution for my use-case, I don't know in advance when the container will be used to run an interactive or non-interactive command. I think this kind of issue will become more common with people start using containers as `toolbox` suggests.

I'm really wondering why this doesn't happen with Docker, I love Podman and I don't really want to fallback to Docker because of that...

Thanks @eriksjolund that's a good starting point. I tried the same and I see that Podman is printing the exact `stty -a` output as it is run from the host system 
```bash
> # Host
> stty -a
speed 38400 baud; rows 51; columns 80; line = 0;
intr = ^C; quit = ^\; erase = ^?; kill = ^U; eof = ^D; eol = <undef>;
```

Interesting finding: Docker acts the same way when both `-t` and `-i` options are present!
```bash
> podman container run --rm -t alpine:latest echo | cat -A
^M$
   > # pressed enter here
> podman container run --rm -ti alpine:latest echo | cat -A
^M$
   > # pressed enter here
> docker container run --rm -t alpine:latest echo | cat -A
^M$
> # pressed enter here
> docker container run --rm -ti alpine:latest echo | cat -A
^M$
   > # pressed enter here
>
```
Does that ring a bell for you?
Replace `--runtime` and see if there is still this problem

Thanks for the suggestion, but it doesn't seem to solve the problem
```bash
> podman container run --rm -t --runtime runc alpine:latest echo | cat -A
^M$
   > # pressed enter here
> podman container run --rm -t --runtime crun alpine:latest echo | cat -A
^M$
   > # pressed enter here
>
```

I think you should not use --tty when outputting to a pipe.

What we could probably do with podman is to have an extension like --tty=auto that sets the tty only when outputting to a tty, but on the other end you could easily wrap this functionality in a script (check if `tty` succeeds and then add `--tty` to the podman command line).

During a very pleasant chat with @giuseppe over IRC, he helped me to implement his suggestion to my context and it works great!
Drop the solution here for the record and hoping it will help others who will struggle with the same issue in the future.
```bash
podman_run () {
    _ti="-ti"; tty 0<&1 &>/dev/null || _ti=""
    podman container run --rm $_ti alpine:latest "$@"
}

> podman_run echo | cat -A
$
> 
```
Feel free to close this issue if this workaround is considered enough, for me it is.

On my machine the only difference I see with `stty -a` is podman has `rows 43; columns 198; ` and docker has `stty: standard input`.

Not sure if this is important.  I would love to understand why we are getting the extra tab.

```
sh-5.1# docker run --rm --tty docker.io/busybox echo hello | cat -A
hello^M$
sh-5.1# podman run --rm --tty docker.io/busybox echo hello | cat -A
hello^M$
        sh-5.1# 
```

Since `docker run -i` causes the same behavior, it looks like podman is doing something in non interactive mode that we should not  do.
```
docker run -i --rm --tty docker.io/busybox echo hello | cat -A
hello^M$
        sh-5.1# 
```

> On my machine the only difference I see with `stty -a` is podman has `rows 43; columns 198; ` and docker has `stty: standard input`.

Same thing here, but I don't think this affects this misbehavior. 

> Not sure if this is important. I would love to understand why we are getting the extra tab.

I'd like to understand the reason behind that too. In the meantime, the workaround suggested by Giuseppe gets the job done ;)

That's not a tab: it's an artifact of CR without NL:
```bash
$ ./bin/podman run --rm --tty docker.io/busybox echo hello hello hello | cat -A
hello hello hello^M$
                    $ 
$
```
